question:
Which of the following features are associated with the architecture of Tughlaq period?  (UPSC Prelims 1991)

Select the correct answer using the codes given below the features:

1. Sloping walls

2. Deliberate attempt to combine the principles of the arch, the lintel and the beam in the buildings

3. Placing the buildings especially the tombs on a high platform

Codes:
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 1 and 3
option_3: 2 and 3
option_4: 1 and 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Features of Hindu influences on Tughlaq architecture include the flat lintel instead of pointed arch, pillars, windows with balconies and eaves and railings.

Tughlaqs built three main types of tombs:

1. square,

2. octagonal and

3. pavilion.

  • It was constructed by Ghiyas-ud-din Tughlaq.
  • A notable feature of the tomb is the sloping walls, at a 75 degree angle with the ground instead of vertical walls. This architectural style is similar to the sloping walls of the Hindola Mahal in Mandu, Madhya Pradesh. It is so called because of the distinctive sloping walls which give an impression that the palace is swaying fromside to side. Perhaps the wall design was intended to buttress the heavy stone arches that support the ceiling. The inside walls are vertical and plain.

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below them:  (UPSC Prelims 1991)

List I

 List II

(Artiste)

(Instrument)

A. Sadiq Ali Khan

 1. Veena

B. V. G. Jog

 2. Sarod

C. Ali Akbar Khan

 3. Flute

D. Pannalal Ghosh

 4. Violin

Select the correct answer from the codes given below:

A B C D
option_1: 1 4 2 3
option_2: 2 1 3 4
option_3: 4 3 1 2
option_4: 1 3 2 4
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Sadiq Ali Khan (1893-1964) : a renowned Veena player, was born in Jaipur. He studied at home mainly under his grandfather Ustad Rajab Ali Khan. He was a court musician of several states including Jhalawar, Alwar and Rampur. He was expert in Alapa and liked depth in music.
  • Vishnu Govind Jog : better known as V. G. Jog (22 February 1922 – 31 January 2004), was an Indian violinist. He was the foremost exponent of the violin in the Hindustani music tradition in the 20th century, and is credited for introducing this instrument into Hindustani music.
  • Ali Akbar Khan (1922 - 2009) : was an Indian classical musician who is famous for his skill with the sarod, a stringed instrument from India. The sarod has 25 strings, 10 of which are plucked with a piece of coconut shell. The remaining strings resonate sympathetically.
  • Khan was a composer, teacher, and virtuoso sarod player who was active in introducing classical Indian music to Western audiences. He is known for bringing worldwide attention to the sarod.
  • Pannalal Ghosh (1911-1960) : was an Indian flute player and composer who is known as the “Pioneer of Indian Classical Flute”. He is credited with making the flute a popular concert instrument in Hindustani classical music.

question: Which one of the following is correctly matched?  (UPSC Prelims 1991)
option_1: Thyagaraja - Tamil
option_2: Purandara Das - Kannada
option_3: Jnaneshwar - Bengali
option_4: Sankaradeva - Gujarati
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Thyagaraja (1767–1847 CE) was one of the greatest composers of Carnatic (South Indian classical) music, but he did not compose in Tamil. He was a Telugu-speaking Brahmin from Tiruvarur in present-day Tamil Nadu.
  • Purandara Dasa (1484–1564 CE) is widely regarded as the Father of Carnatic Music (Sangeeta Pitamaha). He belonged to the Haridasa movement in Karnataka and composed mainly in Kannada.
  • Sant Jnaneshwar (1275–1296 CE), also known as Dnyaneshwar or Jnanadeva, was a famous Marathi saint, philosopher, and poet of the Bhakti movement in Maharashtra.
  • He was a contemporary of Namdev and is best known for his work “Jnaneshwari” (or Dnyaneshwari) — a commentary on the Bhagavad Gita written in Marathi, making it accessible to the common people.
  • Srimanta Sankaradeva (1449–1568 CE) was a great Vaishnava saint, reformer, poet, dramatist, and social leader from Assam in northeast India.
  • He founded the Ekasarana Dharma (a form of Vaishnavism) and used literature, music, and drama to spread devotion to Lord Krishna.
  • His works were composed in Assamese, not Gujarati.

Therefore the correct answer is B.

question: Mitakshara is..........  (UPSC Prelims 1991)
option_1: A work on astronomy
option_2: A treatise on ancient Hindu law of inheritance
option_3: An Agamic text
option_4: A compendium on medicine
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Mitakshara is a Hindu law system that governs inheritance and property rights among members of a Hindu Undivided Family (HUF). It is a legal commentaryon the Yajnavalkya Smriti, written by Vijñāneśvara in the late eleventh and early twelfth century.
  • Mitakshara is commonly followed in the western and southern regions of India. The Mitakshara law of succession applies to the entire country except West Bengal and Assam.
  • Mitakshara is known for its theory of “inheritance by birth”. In the Mitakshara system, sons can claim right to the property during the lifetime of the father.
  • Mitakshara and Dayabhaga are two major schools of Hindu law that have shaped how inheritance laws work in India. Mitakshara was the civil law for upper castes and Dayabhaga was the civil law for lower castes.

question: Of the following centres which is the one where the original monasteries established by Adi Shankaracharya is not found?  (UPSC Prelims 1991)
option_1: Puri
option_2: Kari
option_3: Shringeri
option_4: Dwarka
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

Adi Shankaracharya is said to have established four monasteries in the four directions of India in the ninth century:

1. Jyotirmath: In the north, in Uttara khand

2. Sringeri: In the south

3. Puri: In the east, in Odisha

4. Dwarka: In the west, in Gujarat

  • The monasteries were established to spread the philosophy of Advaita Vedanta and the concept of Sanatana dharma. The monasteries’ establishment was a significant factor in the development of Shankara’s teachings into India’s leading philosophy.
  • The Kanchi Mutt is not one of the four monasteries, but it is still revered as it was the Hindu seer’s home.

Who was Adi Shankaracharya?

  • Adi Shankara (788–820 CE) was an Indian philosopher, theologian, and Vedic scholar who is considered one of the most influential figures in Indian philosophy.
  • Shankara is best known for his philosophy of Advaita Vedanta, which teaches that there is a single reality. His writings focus on the identity of the Self (Atman) and Brahman. He believed that knowledge is the only thing that can save people from suffering.
  • Shankara’s works include commentaries on the Brahma-sutra, the principal Upanishads, and the Bhagavadgita. He is also believed to be an avatar of Lord Shiva and founded four mathas, which are believed to have helped in the historical development of Advaita Vedanta.

question: In his works, Kalidasa exhibits a particular leaning towards the worship of............  (UPSC Prelims 1991)
option_1: Vishnu
option_2: Shiva
option_3: Durga
option_4: Ganesha
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Kalidasa exhibits a particular leaning towards the worship of Shiva.
  • Kalidasa was a Classical Sanskrit author who is often considered ancient India’s greatest poet and playwright. His plays and poetry are primarily based on Hindu Puranas and philosophy.
  • The majority of Kalidasa’s works depict diverse representations of human emotions, connections, and experiences.
  • Themes like love, fidelity, loyalty, honor, and bravery are addressed throughout his plays and poetry.

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists:  (UPSC Prelims 1991)

List I

 List II

        A. Khajuraho temple

 1.Hoysala  style

 

B.Brihadeshwar temple

 2. Pallava style

 

C. Hoyalesvara temple

 3. Chandel

 

D. Seven Pagodas

 4. Chola

 

 

 5. Chedi

Select the correct answer from the codes given below.

A B C D
option_1: 5 1 3 2
option_2: 2 3 1 5
option_3: 3 4 1 2
option_4: 3 4 5 1
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

The Khajuraho temples :

  • It is in Madhya Pradesh, India which is built in the Nagara style. The temples are a UNESCO World Heritage Site and are known for their Nagara-style architectural symbolism and erotic sculptures. The temples were built between 950 and 1050 by the Chandela dynasty.

The temples are divided into three clusters:

1. Eastern,

2. Western, and

3. Southern Khajuraho.

  • The temples are dedicated to Hinduism and Jainism, with the Hindu temples dedicated to Lord Shiva and Lord Vishnu.
  • The temples are made up of a sanctum, a narrow ante-chamber, a transept, additional halls, a mandapa or nave, and an ambulatory passage.

The Brihadeeswara Temple :

  • It was built by Chola emperor Rajaraja I between 1003 and 1010 CE in the Dravidian architectural style. The temple is dedicated to Lord Shiva and is located in Thanjavur, Tamil Nadu, India.
  • The temple is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and is considered a testament to the Chola dynasty’s artistic and architectural achievements. The temple’s walls feature inscriptions that detail the ruler’s gifts to the temple.

The Hoysaleswara Temple in Halebidu :

  • It is an example of Hoysala architecture. Hoysala architecture is a Hindu temple building style that developed between the 11th and 14th centuries in the region of Karnataka. The temple’s architecture is a fusion of Hoysala and Dravidian styles.
  • The Hoysala Empire was founded by Nripa Kama II, who reigned from 1026 CE to 1047 CE. Other examples of Hoysala architecture include the Chennakesava Temple at Belur and the Kesava Temple at Somanathapura. In 2023, these three temples were given UNESCO world heritage site status.

Seven Pagodas :

  • It is also known as Mamallapuram or Mahabalipuram, is a structural temple built in the Dravidian style of architecture. The temple is made of granite blocks and dates back to the 8th century AD.
  • The Pallava dynasty ruled the area from the 6th to 9th centuries CE and built the monuments at Mamallapuram.
  • The monuments include rock-cut cave temples, monolithic free-standing rathas, open air-bas-relief, and structural temples. The Shore Temple is a free-standing rock cut structural temple in the Dravidian style. Dravidian temples are usually enclosed by an outer wall with a large gateway tower. The Shore Temple has an octagonal neck topped by a round finial.

question: Who is the well-known collector of artifacts and curios?  (UPSC Prelims 1991)
option_1: Salar Jung
option_2: Wadeyar
option_3: Rabindranath Tagore
option_4: Motilal Nehru
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Salar Jung was a renowned collector of art, antiques, and curiosities from across the world. He served as the Prime Minister of the Nizam of Hyderabad and spent a large part of his life gathering rare artifacts, sculptures, manuscripts, clocks, paintings, and furniture from various countries. His vast personal collection later became the foundation of the Salar Jung Museum in Hyderabad, one of the largest art museums in India. 
  • The Wodeyars of Mysore were royal patrons of art and architecture but not famous individual collectors. 
  • Rabindranath Tagore was a great poet, philosopher, and painter but not known for collecting artifacts.
  • Motilal Nehru was a political leader and freedom fighter, not associated with art collection. 
  • Hence, the correct answer is Salar Jung, whose name is synonymous with India’s richest collection of global antiques and artistic treasures.

question: Roman settlement in India is found at.......... (UPSC Prelims 1991)
option_1: Ujjain
option_2: Kshudrakas
option_3: Arikmedu
option_4: Koshambi
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Arikamedu is a Roman settlement in India. It’s located in Kakkayanthope, four kilometers from Puducherry town. Arikamedu, which means “eroding mud”.
  • The city is located along the beautiful Ariyankuppam river and is believed to be the only city with an archaeological record of Roman presence in India.
  • Arikamedu was a Chola port city from the 1st century BC. It was a fishing village that traded with Roman traders and made beads. The site is mentioned in the Periplus of the Erythraean Sea, a 1st century CE Greco-Roman text that describes the Indian Subcontinent’s coastline.

question: Painted greyware is attributed to..............  (UPSC Prelims 1991)
option_1: Vedic period
option_2: Guptas
option_3: Mauryas
option_4: Indus valley civilization
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Painted Grey Ware (PGW) is associated with the Vedic period, which is the beginning of the Iron Age. The PGW culture likely corresponds to the middle and late Vedic period, which is the Kuru Panchala kingdom. The Kuru-Panchala kingdom was the first large state in the Indian subcontinent after the Indus Valley civilization declined.
  • PGW is considered the characteristic pottery of the Rig Vedic era. The Rig Vedic people were only familiar with PGW pottery, while the Later Vedic people were familiar with PGW, Black and Red Ware,Black Slipped Ware, and Red Ware. PGW pottery has been found in excavations at Hastinapura and Atranjikhera. Bhagawanpura is also a PGW site.

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists:  (UPSC Prelims 1991)

 

List-1

 

List-II

A.

Classical music

1.

Lata Mangeshkar, Mukesh, Manna Dey

B.

Light classical music

2.

Bade Gulam Ali Khan, Amir Khan, Jasraj

C.

Light music

3.

Siddeswari Devi, Rasodan Bai, Girija Devi

D.

Folk music

4.

Teejan Bai, Sheikh Gulab, Poorna Das

Select the correct answer from the codes given below.

A B C D
option_1: 1 2 3 4
option_2: 2 3 1 4
option_3: 3 1 2 4
option_4: 1 3 2 4
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Classical music corresponds to Bade Ghulam Ali Khan, Amir Khan, and Pandit Jasraj (Option 2) — all of whom are renowned exponents of Hindustani classical music. Bade Ghulam Ali Khan was a master of the Patiala Gharana, Amir Khan was the founder of the Indore Gharana, and Pandit Jasraj represented the Mewati Gharana. Their work reflects the pure traditional form of Indian classical music based on ragas.
  • Light classical music matches with Siddheshwari Devi, Rasoolan Bai, and Girija Devi (Option 3) — all famous for semi-classical forms such as Thumri, Dadra, and Kajri, which blend classical technique with a more expressive, lyrical style.
  • Light music aligns with Lata Mangeshkar, Mukesh, and Manna Dey (Option 1) — legendary playback singers of Indian cinema, known for popular film songs and modern compositions that are melodious but not strictly classical.
  • Finally, Folk music fits with Teejan Bai, Sheikh Gulab, and Poorna Das (Option 4) — well-known folk singers representing regional traditions. Teejan Bai is famous for Pandavani from Chhattisgarh, Sheikh Gulab for Bihar’s folk songs, and Poorna Das for Bengali Baul music.

Hence, the correct matching is — A–2, B–3, C–1, D–4, which corresponds to option (b).

question: Which one of the following is a historical work by Hemachandra? (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Dvasraya-kavya
option_2: Prabandha chintamani
option_3: Kamataka bhasa bhushana
option_4: Samarangana sutradhara
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

 Acharya Hemachandra was a Jain scholar, poet, and polymath who wrote several historical works, including:

1. Kumarpal Charita: A biography of the Indian king Kumarapala

2. Trishashti shalaka purusha charitra: An epic Sanskrit poem about the lives of 63 Jain figures

3. Dvyashraya Kavya: An epic about the history of the Chaulukya dynasty

  • Hemachandra also wrote Sanskrit and Prakrit grammars, textbooks on science and Indian philosophy, and several other poems.

Prabandha Chintamani :

  • It is a collection of semi-historical biographical narratives, or prabandhas, written in Sanskrit. It was compiled in 1304 CE by Jain scholar Merutunga in the Vaghela kingdom, which is present-day Gujarat.
  • The book is an important text in Jain literature and provides details about the political and cultural life of the 14th century. It also includes dates for the accession of the kings of Anhilvada over a period of about 400 years.

Karnataka Bhasha Bhushana :

  • It is a 12th century work on Kannada grammar by Nagavarmma. The title translates to “Ornament of the Karnataka language”.
  • Nagavarma is considered the greatest Kannada grammarian and is also known for his works Kavyavalokana, Vastukosa, and Varadhamanapurana. Karnataka Bhasha Bhushana is a 170-page hardcover book published in 2020 by Lewis Rice. It includes 280 sutras and romanized verses with appended Kannada.

Samarangana Sutradhara :

  • It is an 11th-century poetic treatise on classical Indian architecture. The title translates literally to “an architect of human dwellings”.
  • The treatise is written in Sanskrit and attributed to the Paramar King Bhoja of Dhar, Madhya Pradesh. It is composed in poetic verses, detailing the theory and practice of Hindu temple architecture in north, west and south India.

The treatise covers a wide range of topics, including:

  • Town planning, House architecture, Temple architecture, Sculpture, Iconography, Iconometry, Iconoplastic art, Mudras, Creation-theory, Geography.
  • The treatise is made up of 83 chapters, 7,500 verses, and an introduction, Sanskrit text, and verse-by-verse English translation.

question:
Given below are four wonders not usually included among the seven wonders of the world............ (UPSC Prelims 1992)

1. The Great Wall of China

2. The Taj Mahal at Agra

3. The Leaning Tower of Pisa

4. The Angkor Vat Temple in Kampuchea

What is the correct chronological sequence in which they were constructed?

Choose the answer from the codes given below.
option_1: 3, 2, 1 and 4
option_2: 1, 4, 3 and 2
option_3: 1, 3, 4 and 2
option_4: 4, 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  •  The Great Wall of China : In 220 BC, under Qin Shi Huang, sections of earlier fortifications were joined together to form a united defence system against invasions from the north.
  •  Taj Mahal : Taj Mahal, the pinnacle of Mughal architecture. It was commissioned in 1632 by the Mughal emperor, Shah Jahan (reigned 1628-1658), to house the tomb of his favourite wife, Mumtaz Mahal.
  •  The Leaning Tower of Pisa : Construction of the tower occurred in three stages over 199 years. Work on the ground floor of the white marble campanile began on August 14, 1173.
  •  Angkor Wat : The initial design and construction of the temple took place in the first half of the 12th century, during the reign of Suryavarman II ruled (1113-1150 AD). Dedicated to Vishnu, it was built as the king’s state temple and capital city.

question: Which one of the following pairs of famous sayings and their sources is correctly matched? (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Vasudhaiva Kutumbhakam: Artha Sastra
option_2: Yogakshemam vahamyaham: Rig Veda
option_3: Satyameva jayate : Mundaka Upanishad
option_4: Satyam Shivam Sundaram: Bhagavat Gita
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

“Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam” :

  • It is an ancient Indian phrase that means “the world is one family”. It has been a guiding principle for the Indian family system since ancient times. The phrase has become amantra in India’s diplomatic lexicon, and many Indian leaders, including Jawaharlal Nehru and Narendra Modi, have used it to explain the country’s global outlook.
  • The phrase is from the Maha Upanishad. The theme and the logo for India’s G20 Presidency from December 1, 2022, till November 30, 2023 has a mention of “Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam” or “One Earth One Family-One Future”.

“Yogakshemam Vahamyaham” :

  • It is a Sanskrit slogan that means “Your welfare is our responsibility”. It is the guiding philosophy of the Life Insurance Corporation of India (LIC). The slogan is derived from the 22nd verse of the 9th chapter of the Bhagavad Gita. In the verse, Lord Krishna says, “I personally take care of the well-being and protection of those who wholeheartedly surrender to me with unwavering faith”.

Satyameva Jayate :

  • The national motto of India, which translates to “truth alone triumphs”. It is a part of a mantra from the Hindu scripture Mundaka Upanishad, the concluding part of the sacred Hindu Vedas. The motto is inscribed in Devanagari script at the base of the Lion Capital of Ashoka and is an integral part of the Indian national emblem. It was adopted as the national motto of India on January 26, 1950, the day India became a republic.

Satyam Shivam Sundaram :

  • It is a phrase that means truth, goodness, and beauty. It is a core value in the Vedas, Upnishads, Epics, and Puranas. It is said that truth, beauty, and joy are the forms of divinity.

question: Thillana is a format of............ (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Kathak
option_2: Kuchipudi
option_3: Odissi
option_4: Bharatnatyam
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Thillana is a format of Bharatanatyam, a classical Indian dance form that originated in Tamil Nadu. It is a rhythmic dance that is performed at the end of a Bharatanatyam recital.
  • Thillana is a pure dance form that focuses on the use of intricate footwork and rhythmic movements. It is a presentation style in Bharatanatyam where a host of dancers perform together, exploring the extent of strong kinetics. This style requires exemplary skills in terms of rhythm, timing, and synchronization.
  • Thillana is a small composition sung towards the end of a Carnatic concert. It is one of the most anticipated pieces in the recital. Thillanas are popular because of their use of Jathis, which are syllables with fast pace and mathematical patterns.

Kathak :

  • It is one of the eight major forms of Indian classical dance. The origin of Kathak is traditionally attributed to the traveling bards in ancient northern India known as Kathakar or storytellers.
  • The term Kathak is derived from the Sanskrit word Katha which means “story”, and Kathakar which means “the one who tells a story”, or “to do with stories”.
  • Wandering Kathakar communicated stories from the great epics and ancient mythology through dance, songs and music.
  • Kathak dancers tell various stories through their hand movements and extensive footwork, their body movements and f lexibility but most importantly through their facial expressions. Kathak often has a strong beat, and can be danced in many taals. Kathak evolved during the Bhakti movement, particularly by incorporating the childhood and stories of the Hindu god Krishna, as well as independently in the courts of north Indian kingdoms.
  • During the period of Mughal rule, the emperors were patrons of Kathak dance and actively promoted it in their royal courts.
  • Kathak performances include Urdu ghazaland commonly used instruments brought during the Mughal period. As a result, it is the only Indian classical dance form to feature Persian elements.

Kuchipudi :

  • It is a classical Indian dance form that combines Nritta (pure dance), Nritya (expressional dance), and Natya (drama). It’s characterized by intricate footwork, graceful movements, and subtle facial expressions.
  • Kuchipudi is named after the village of Kuchipudi in Andhra Pradesh, where it was f irst performed. It originated in the 17th century with the creation of the dance drama Bhama Kalapam by Sidhyendra Yogi. The dance-drama is about Satyabhāma, the jealous wife of the god Krishna.
  • Kuchipudi is different from the other five classical dance styles in India because it includes singing. It was originally only practiced by men, but later women were allowed to participate.

Odissi :

  • It is a classical Indian dance form that originated in the temples of Odisha. It is one of the eight classical dances of India.
  • Odissi is characterized by its graceful movements and lyrical quality. It is usually performed to Orissa classical music, a traditional style of Indian classical music that originated in the state of Odisha.
  • Odissi is based on the classical Natya Shastra and the Abhinaya Darpana. It is considered a dance of love that touches on the human and divine aspects of life.
  • Odissi dance is considered to be one of the most complex forms of classical dances. It keeps different body parts in sync and strengthens the lower half of your body, especially muscles of your thighs and calves.

Some features of Odissi dance include:

1. Hasta mudra: Or hand gestures play a significant role in Odissi dance.

2. Rhythm: The rhythm is referred to as tala in India.

3. Body movement: Body movement and facial expression help to express the narrative.

  • The earliest form of Odissi is said to be the Odhra Magadha, which is a southeastern style of classical dance.

Bharatanatyam :

  • It is a classical dance form from Tamil Nadu, India. It is known for its grace, purity, and sculpturesque poses. Bharatanatyam is recognized by the Sangeet Natak Akademi and expresses South Indian religious themes and spiritual ideas, particularly of Shaivism and in general of Hinduism.

question: The well known painting, Bani Thani’ belongs to the............. (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Kangra School
option_2: Kishangarh School
option_3: Bundi School
option_4: Jaipur School
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The painting “Bani Thani” is part of the Kishangarh School of Indian painting. The Kishangarh School emerged in the 18th century in the Princely State of Kishangarh, which is located in Central Rajasthan.
  • The Kishangarh style of painting is a combination of Mughal and local styles. The theme of this style is the representation of the affection between Krishna and Radha.
  • “Bani Thani” is an Indian miniature painting created by Nihal Chand from the Marwar school of Kishangarh. The painting depicts a woman who is elegant and graceful. The subject of the painting, Bani Thani, was a singer and poet in Kishangarh during the rule of Sawant Singh. Her portrait is often compared with that of the Mona Lisa.
  • The artists used paper, fine cloth, wooden base, marble, ivory panels, and even leather for this art. On any of these mediums, Bani Thani is painted using vegetable and minerals colors, and even pure silver and gold is used to make the miniature rich and abundant.

Kangra painting :

  • It is the pictorial art of Kangra, named after the Kangra State, a former princely state of Himachal Pradesh, which patronized the art. It became prevalent with the fading of Basohli school of painting in mid 18th century, and soon produced such a magnitude in paintings both in content as well as volume, that the Pahari painting school, came to be known as Kangra paintings.
  • Pahari paintings, as the name suggests, were paintings executed in the hilly regions of India, in the sub-Himalayan state of Himachal Pradesh. It is in the development and modification of Pahari paintings, that the Kangra School features. Under the patronage of Maharaja Sansar Chand (c.1765–1823), it became the most important center of Pahari painting.

The Bundi School :

  • It is an important school of the Rajasthani style of Indian miniature painting that lasted from the 17th to the end of the 19th century in this princely state of Bundi ruled by Hadoti Rajputs.
  • One of the earliest examples of the Bundi Paintings is the Chunar Ragamala painted in 1561. Bundi paintings emphasized on hunting, court scenes, festivals, processions, life of nobles, lovers, animals, birds and scenes from Lord Krishna’s life.

The Jaipur School of painting :

  • It is a Rajasthani school of painting that originated in the early 17th century in the old capital of the state of Amber. The school’s art developed between the late 16th and early 18th century and has elements of both the Rajasthani style and the Mughal style.
  • The Jaipur School inherited the cultural legacy of the Amber style. The art developed under the close affiliation between the rulers of Jaipur and the Mughals. The artists painted sets based on Rasikapriya, Gita Govinda, Baramasa and Ragamala, where the hero’s figure is in striking resemblance with the king.
  • The school reached its apex under the reign of Sawai Pratap Singh in the 18th century. Suratkhana, or the painting department, created miniatures for the Bhagavata Purana, Ramayana, Ragamala, and many other portraits.

question: Gupta architecture is represented by many brick temples and the temple of Bhitargaon is most notable for its well preserved and moulded bricks of excellent design, this temple has.............. (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: A tall Gopuram
option_2: A Pyramidal roof
option_3: An Apsidal plan
option_4: Double basement
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Bhitargaon Temple is a terraced brick building fronted with a terracotta panel. Built in the 5th century during the Gupta period, it is the oldest remaining brick/ terracotta Hindu shrine with a pyramidal roof and a high shikhara, though its upper chamber did sustain some damage in the 18th century.

question: The Chishti saint, in whose honour ‘Urs’ is held in Ajmer every year, was.............. (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Nizamuddin Auliya
option_2: Moin-ud-din
option_3: Nasiruddin Chiragh-i-Delhi
option_4: Hamidud-Din Nagori
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Chishti saint in whose honor the annual Urs festival is held in Ajmer is Khwaja Moinuddin Chishti.
  • The festival commemorates the death anniversary of the Sufi saint, who lived from 1143–1236.
  • He is also known as Khwaja Gharib Nawaz and is considered the founder of the Chishtiya Sufi order in India.
  • The festival is celebrated with night-long qawaali sessions and prayer meetings at the Dargah and in homes.

question: The earliest rock-cut caves in western India are those at............. (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Nasik, Ellora and Ajanta
option_2: Junnar, Kalyan and Pitalkhora
option_3: Ajanta, Bhaja and Kondane
option_4: Bhala, Pitalkhora and Kondane
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The earliest rock-cut caves in western India are located at Ajanta, Bhaja, and Kondane. The Ajanta Caves are a group of 29 Buddhist caves that date back to the 2nd century BC. The caves are located near the village of Ajanta in the Aurangabad district of Maharashtra.
  • The Ajanta Caves are Buddhist rock-cut cave temples and monasteries that are known for their wall paintings. The caves were occupied by Buddhist monks from 200 BCE to 650 AD.
  • After about 850 years of continuous occupation, the caves were abandoned following the decline of Buddhism in the region.
  • Other early cave temples include the Bhaja Caves, the Karla Caves, the Bedse Caves, and the Kanheri Caves.

question: In Mughal paintings one notices the adoption of principle of foreshortening whereby near and distant people and things could be placed in perspective. This was due to the influence of the............. (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: British
option_2: Dutch
option_3: Portuguese
option_4: Danish
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The principle of foreshortening was adopted in Mughal paintings due to the influence of European painting.
  • Foreshortening is a technique that compresses long objects to make them appear shorter, creating the illusion of depth. Renaissance artists used foreshortening to create naturalistic art.

The Mughals adopted many art techniques from the Europeans, including:

1. Foreshortening

2. Pietradura

3. Divinity concept

4. Three-dimensional techniques

5. Light and shade

  • The Mughals also drew from Persian antecedents, bringing the technique of foreshortening to Indian painting.

question:
Which one of the following pairs of kings of ancient and medieval periods of Indian history and the works authored by them is correctly matched?  (UPSC Prelims 1993)

(a)

Krishna devaraya

Samaranganasutradhara

(b)

Mahendra varman

Mattavilasaprahasana

(c)

 Bhojadeva

 Manasollasa

(d)

 Somesvara

 Amuktamalayada
option_1:
Krishna devaraya

Samaranganasutradhara
option_2:
Mahendra varman

Mattavilasaprahasana
option_3:
Bhojadeva

 Manasollasa
option_4:
Somesvara

 Amuktamalayada
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

Samarangana Sutradhara :

  • It is a 11th-century poetic treatise written in Sanskrit by Paramara King Bhoja of Dhar, who lived from 1000-1055 AD. The work is an encyclopedic study of classical Indian architecture, also known as Vastu Shastra. The word Samarangana literally means "architect of human dwellings" and refers to the planning of towns and villages, the construction of houses, halls, palaces, and various machines.

Mattavilasaprahasana :

  • Mahendravarman I (571-630CE) wrote the Sanskrit play Mattavilasa Prahasana in the early seventh century. The play's title translates to "A Farce of Drunken Sport".
  • Mattavilasa Prahasana is a Buddhist text that discusses the decline of Buddhism in Tamizhakam. Mahendravarman I was a Pallava King who also expanded the Pallava Kingdom and introduced many ornate temples and monuments in southern India.

The Mänasollāsa:

  • It is also known as Abhilashitartha
  • Chintamani, is a Sanskrit text written by the Kalyani Chalukya king Someshvara III in the early 12th century. The book is also known as a manual of instruction for kings,
  • Someshvara III ruled over the Karnataka-Andhra regions from 1126-1138. The book includes a mention of the Chitrakathi tradition, which is the use of wooden puppets to tell the stories of the Ramayana and Mahabharata.

Amuktamalayada:

  • Krishnadevaraya, the ruler of the
  • Vijayanagara Empire, wrote the Telugu epic poem Amuktamalyada in the early 16th century. The title translates to "One who offered the garland after wearing it himself".
  • Krishnadevaraya was the third ruler of the Tuluva Dynasty and is considered to be its greatest ruler. He reigned from 1509-1529 and was a polyglot, fluent in Kannada, Sanskrit, Telugu, and Tamil.
  • Amuktamalyada is a long poem of about 875 verses that brings together several diverse themes.

question: Which one of the following monuments figured in the news recently in connection with a fall of some of its parts and its consequent archaeological conservation?  (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Jagannath Temple, Puri
option_2: Sun Temple, Konark
option_3: Khandariya Mahadeva Temple, Khajuraho
option_4: Chaunsat Yogini Temple, Bheraghat
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

Jagannath Temple :

  • It is an important Hindu temple in Puri, Odisha, that is dedicated to Jagannath, a form of Vishnu. The temple is part of the Char Dham pilgrimages, which also include Badrinath, Dwaraka, and Rameswaram.
  • The state government of Odisha has decided to use facial recognition technology based on artificial intelligence (AI) at the Jagannath Temple in Puri to prevent stampedes and track criminals.

The Konark Sun Temple :

  • The Konark Sun Temple is also known as the Black Pagoda. It is a 13th-century Hindu temple in Konark, Odisha, India. The temple is dedicated to the Hindu sun god Surya, and is considered a pinnacle of Hindu.

Orissan architecture :

  • The temple’s name comes from the Sanskrit words Kona (angle) and Arka (sun). The temple is associated with the legend of Samba, the son of Krishna, who was cured of leprosy by the sun god’s blessings.
  • The temple is known for its sculptural innovations and the quality of its carvings. It features 24 wheels, representing the wheels of Surya’s sun chariot. The 12 wheels represent the 12 months of the year, and the 8 spokes represent the 8 prahars or time divisions of the day.

Kandariya Mahadeva Temple :

  • It is a Hindu temple in the Khajuraho Group of Monuments in the Chhatarpur district of Madhya Pradesh, India. Built between 1025-1050 AD, the temple is the largest and most ornate in the medieval temple group. It’s also known as the “Great God of the Cave”.
  • The temple is dedicated to Lord Shiva and is considered one of the best examples of temples preserved from the medieval period in India. It’s built in the shape of a steep mountain, with 84 mini shikharas on the roof to represent the minor mountain peaks around Mount Meru.

The temple’s main features include:

1. A marble shivalingam in the sanctum

2. About 870 sculptures, including erotic statues

3. Nagara-style architectural symbolism

  • The temple complex is spread over 6 square kilometers (2.3 sq mi) in the Khajuraho village, to the west of the Vishnu temple.

Chausath Yogini Temple :

  • It is also known as the Golaki Math, is a Hindu temple in Bhedaghat, Madhya Pradesh. It is a popular heritage tourism attraction and is often considered part of Jabalpur sightseeing.
  • The temple is a circular structure with a diameter of 130 feet and an inner diameter of 116 meters. It has 64 small shrines, each dedicated to one of the yoginis. The main shrine is dedicated to the goddess Durga, who is considered to be the chief among the yoginis. The temple also has 84 cells that form a full circle.
  • The temple is built in the 11th century A.D. and is located on a hilltop in Bhedaghat. Around 150 steep steps lead to the shrine, which is at a good height.

question: Consider the following passage: “In the course of a career on the road spanning almost thirty years, he crossed the breadth of the Eastern hemisphere, visited territories equivalent to about 44 modern countries, and put behind him a a total distance of approximately 73,000 miles.” The world’s greatest traveller of pre-modern times to whom the above passage refers is..............  (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Megasthenes
option_2: Fa-Hien
option_3: Marco Polo
option_4: Ibn Battuta
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The passage refers to Marco Polo, one of the first and most famous Europeans to travel to Asia during the Middle Ages.
  • The Eastern Hemisphere is the half of the planet Earth that is east of the prime meridian and west of the antimeridian. It is also used to refer to Afro-Eurasia and Australia.

question: The statue of Gomateswara at Sravanabelagola was built by............ (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Chandragupta Maurya
option_2: Kharavela
option_3: Amoghavarsha
option_4: Chamundaraya
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The statue of Gomateshwara at Shravanabelagola was built by Chavundaraya, a minister in the Ganga dynasty, in 983 CE. The statue is 57 feet tall and is carved from a single block of granite. It is located on the top of Vindhyagiri Hill.
  • The statue is considered one of the world’s tallest free-standing monolithic statues. It was the tallest Jain statue until 2016.
  • The statue is also known as Bahubali, and is a major pilgrimage site for Jains.
  • According to Jainism, Bahubali was the second of the hundred sons of Rishabha, the first Tirthankara, and king of Podanpur.

question: Which one of the following is a language of Baluchistan but linguistically Dravidian? (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Brahui
option_2: Kui
option_3: Parji
option_4: Pengo
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Brahui is a Dravidian language spoken by the Brahui people, who are mainly found in the Pakistani province of Balochistan. Brahui is the only Dravidian language spoken in Pakistan.
  • Brahui is a far northwestern member of the Dravidian family of languages. It has integrated elements from Persian, Sindhi, Balochi, and other languages.
  • Small groups of Brahui speakers are also found in Irani Balochistan, Afghanistan, and in and around the Marw oasis in Turkmenistan.

question: Vidhushaka, a common character in Sanskrit drama, is invariably a............ (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Brahmana
option_2: Kshatriya
option_3: Vaisya
option_4: Shudra
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • In Sanskrit drama, the character Vidushaka is usually a male Brahmin. The Vidushaka is a witty and humorous companion to the main characters. They are often depicted as a dwarf-like figure with a tuft on their head and a staff in their hand. 
  • The Vidushaka is a clown or jester character. They are used to introduce subjects background of a story, or to portray events that are otherwise forbidden by protocol on stage.
  • The Vidushaka is usually a confident attendant of the hero.
  • They are educated in Sanskrit, but speak in Prakrit, the vernacular language of the time.
  • The Vidushaka is a cheerful, amusing, and often mirthful friend of the hero. They are liked by the female attendants of the queen.

question: “Live well, as long as you live. Live well even by borrowings, for, once cremated, there is no ‘return.” This rejection of after-life in an aphorism of the............. (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Kapalika sect
option_2: Sunyavada of Nagarjuna
option_3: Ajivikas
option_4: Charvakas
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Charvaka, also known as Lokayata, is an ancient school of Indian materialism. Charvaka holds direct perception, empiricism, and conditional inference as proper sources of knowledge, embraces philosophical skepticism and rejects ritualism, and supernaturalism.

The Kapalika sect :

  • They were a group of Shaivite ascetics who practiced a tantric form of Saivism from the 8th to the 13th century. The name “Kapalika” literally means “skull-men” because they carried a skull-topped staff and a cranium begging bowl.
  • The Kapalika sect are known for their esoteric rituals, which allegedly included animal and human sacrifice. However, there is no evidence for the latter. Some members of the Kapalika-s are thought to have written the Bhairava Tantra-s, including the Kaula Tantra-s subdivision.The Kapalika sect are one of the more radical sects to have existed.

Sunyavada, or the “Doctrine of Void”:

  • It is the philosophy of Nagarjuna, a Buddhist monk who lived from 150 to 250 CE. Sunyavada is a major school of Buddhism, and Nagarjuna is considered the first to systematically expound it.
  • Nagarjuna’s philosophy is based on the Buddhist theory of “dependent origination” (pratitya-samutpada). This doctrine states that whatever exists, exists as being dependent on its causes and conditions. Nagarjuna’s central concept is the “emptiness (sunyata) of all things (dharmas)”. This concept points to the ever-changing and therefore never fixed nature of all phenomena.
  • Nagarjuna’s arguments cover a number of topics, including knowledge.

He listed four epistemic instruments:

1. perception,

2. inference,

3. recognition of likeness, and

4. something else.

  • According to Kumarajiva, Nagarjuna was born at the foot of an Arjuna tree to a brahmin family in South India. He excelled in the traditional religious and secular subjects studied by the Indian priestly caste and by the age of twenty was renowned for his learning.

The Ajivikas :

  • They were an ancient Indian philosophical sect and ascetic movement that lasted until the 14th century. The Ajivikas believed that everything is predetermined by fate, and that human actions have no consequence. They also believed in karma and liberation.
  • The Ajivikas were a rival to Buddhism and Jainism, and were popular during the reign of the Mauryan Emperor Bindusara. The Ajivikas believed in rebirth and a doctrine of reanimation, which is the idea that one person’s soul can be reanimated in the dead bodies of others.
  • The Ajivikas were founded by Goshala Maskariputra, a friend of Mahavira, the 24th Tirthankara. The name Ajivika may mean “following the ascetic way of life”.

question: The caves and rock-cut temples at Ellora are............. (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Buddhist
option_2: Buddhist and Jain
option_3: Hindu and Jain
option_4: Hindu, Buddhist and Jain
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The caves and rock-cut temples at Ellora are Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain.
  • The Ellora Caves are a multi-religious complex of over 100 caves, 34 of which are open to the public. The caves were built during the Satavahana period, which is part of the Vedic Dynasty. The caves were constructed close together to illustrate the religious harmony that existed in ancient India.
  • The caves are affiliated with three of India’s religious traditions:

1. Hinduism

2. Buddhism and

3. Jainism

  • The 12 Buddhist caves were built during the 5th-7th century, and the five Jain caves belong to the 9th and 10th centuries.
  • The Ellora Caves are considered one of the finest examples of rock-cut architecture and were declared a World Heritage Site in 1983.

question: Prem Vatika, poems on the life of Krishna, was composed by............. (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Bihari
option_2: Surdas
option_3: Raskhan
option_4: Kabir
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The poet Raskhan composed the poems Prem Vatika, which are about the life and divine love of Lord Krishna. Raskhan was a16th-century poet who wrote primarily in Braj Bhasha, a dialect of Hindi.
  • Raskhan’s real name was Syed Ibrahim Khan. He became a devotee of Lord Krishna in his early life and wrote many things inspired by him. He spent his entire life in Brindavan and died around 1628 A.D..

question: Which one of the following temples figured in the news regarding the institution of the Devadasis? (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Jagannath temple, Puri
option_2: Pasupatinath temple, Kathmandu
option_3: Kandariyamahadev temple, Khajuraho
option_4: Chaunsathyogini temple, Bheraghat
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Jagannath Temple in Puri has been historically associated with the institution of the Devadasis. The Devadasi system became prevalent from the Chola period, when temples became large complexes that required a large number of permanent women workers.
  • In the Jagannath Temple, Devadasis would dance in the main hall after Lord Jagannatha gave Darshana to the devotees. The Devadasis would perform pure dance, accompanied by musicians and the Rajaguru (the court guru). The audience could watch the Devadasis dance.
  • The Jagannath Temple administration attempted to enroll fresh Devadasis to keep the 800-year-old tradition alive, but the effort failed due to nationwide protest and because no girl volunteered. In 2021, Parasmani Devi, the last surviving “devadasi” of the Shri Jagannath Temple, died at the age of 90.

Jagannath Temple :

  • It is an important Hindu temple in Puri, Odisha, that is dedicated to Jagannath, a form of Vishnu. The temple is part of the Char Dham pilgrimages, which also include Badrinath, Dwaraka, and Rameswaram.
  • The state government of Odisha has decided to use facial recognition technology based on artificial intelligence (AI) at the Jagannath Temple in Puri to prevent stampedes and track criminals.

Pashupatinath Temple :

  • It is a Hindu temple in Kathmandu, Nepal. It’s located on both sides of the Bagmati River, on the eastern outskirts of Kathmandu.
  • The temple is dedicated to Pashupati, a form of Shiva. It is considered one of the holiest Hindu shrines in Nepal and was classified as a World Heritage Site in 1979.

Kandariya Mahadeva Temple :

  • It is a Hindu temple in the Khajuraho Group of Monuments in the Chhatarpur district of Madhya Pradesh, India. The temple is dedicated to Lord Shiva and is known for its elaborate Nagara style architecture.
  • The temple’s name means “the Great God of the Cave”. It’s the largest and most ornate temple in the medieval temple group, and is considered one of the best examples of medieval temples in India. The temple is built in the shape of a steep mountain, with 84 mini shikharas on its roof representing the minor peaks around Mount Meru. The temple’s 30.5-meter-long structure was built between 1025 and 1050 and represents the high point of Chandela architecture.

The Chausath Yogini temple in Morena :

  • It is an 11th-century temple located in Mitaoli village, 40 km from Gwalior. The temple is also known as Ekattarso Mahadeva Temple.
  • According to an inscription from 1323 CE, the temple was built by King Devapala of the Kachchhapaghata dynasty. The temple is made of sand and granite stones and has a circular shape with a 130 m outer wall and 116 m inner wall. Some say it is a beautiful piece of heritage and a good place to visit.

question: According to ancient Indian cosmogonic ideas the sequential order of the cycle of four acons (Yugas) is............ (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Dvapara, Krita, Treta and Kali
option_2: Krita, Dvapara, Treta and Kali
option_3: Krita, Treta, Dvapara and Kali
option_4: Treta, Dvapara, Kali and Krita
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • According to ancient Indian cosmogonic ideas, the sequential order of the cycle of four aeons (yugas) is Krita, Treta, Dvapara, and Kali.

Krita Yuga :

  • It is the first and best of the four yugas, or world ages, in a Yuga Cycle. It is also known as Satya Yuga, or the Golden Age.
  • Krita Yuga lasts for 1,728,000 years, with 400 years each of the beginning and end transitions (sandhyas), making it 4,800 years.
  • Krita Yuga is an age of perfection, truth, and goodness. It is named as such because there was only one religion, all men were saintly, and therefore they were not required to perform religious ceremonies.
  • During Krita Yuga, the process of self realization is meditation on Visnu. The average life span at the beginning of the yuga is 100,000 years.

Treta Yuga :

  • It is the second of four yugas in a Yuga Cycle. The word “Treta Yuga” means “three collections” or “age of three”. It lasts for 1,296,000 years, which is three times the length of the Kali Yuga.
  • Treta Yuga is preceded by the Satya Yuga and followed by the Dvapara Yuga. According to Hindu mythology, the events of the Ramayana take place in Treta Yuga.

Other important events that occur during Treta Yuga include:

1. The appearance of agriculture and mining

2. The rise of Lord Rama as a beacon of righteousness

3. The appearance of three avatars of Lord Vishnu, i) Vamana, ii) Parashurama, and iii) Rama, respectively

4. The building of great and tall stone structures across the earth

Dvapara Yuga :

  • It is the third and third-best of the four yugas in a Yuga Cycle. It lasts for 864,000 years.
  • According to the Puranas, this yuga ended when Krishna returned to his eternal home in Vaikuntha.

Here are some other characteristics of Dvapara Yuga:

1. Religion: Religion is based on two principles: honesty and compassion.

2. Energy: This age is referred to as the age of energy because in this age we are better able to understand and har ness energy than in Kali Yuga.

3. Technology: Technology is booming and all sorts of new religions, philos ophies, and scientific discoveries are popping up.

4. Ego: The ego in Dvapara Yuga is en ergized.

Kali Yuga :

  • It is the fourth and final Yuga in the cycle of ages. It is believed to have begun in 3102 BCE and last for 432,000 years.
  • Kali Yuga is known as the age of darkness, ignorance, and devastation. It is believed to be the present age, full of conflict and sin.

Some characteristics of the Kali Yuga include:

 1. An increase in diseases and disorders

2. People accept harsh and cruel conditions as basic to life

3. People will indulge in trade and commercial activity, even though having the outward appearance of saints

4. A man will be friends only with his wife

5. People will exhibit ingenuity and skill in engineering

  • Near the end of Kali Yuga, a cataclysm and a re-establishment of dharma occur to usher in the next cycle’s Krita (Satya) Yuga.

question: The term apabhramsa was used in medieval Sanskrit texts to denote.............. (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Outcastes among the Rajputs
option_2: Deviations from Vedic rituals
option_3: Early forms of some of the modern Indian languages
option_4: Non-Sanskrit verse metres
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The term apabhramsa was used in medieval Sanskrit texts to describe the Prakrit languages that evolved from Sanskrit. The term comes from Sanskrit, where “apa” means “away” or “off” and “bhramsa” means “deviation” or “corruption”.
  • Apabhramsa is a term that is collectively provided to the dialects that deviate from Sanskrit. It literally means “corrupt” or “non-grammatical language”, that which deviates from the norm of Sanskrit grammar.
  • Apabhramsa was used to denote the forms of some modern Indian languages spoken in North India. It was the most vibrant language of colloquial and literary works in North India.

question: Which one of the following sculptures invariably used green schist as the medium? (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Maurya sculptures
option_2: Mathura sculptures
option_3: Bharhut sculptures
option_4: Gandhara sculptures
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

Mauryan Scupltures : 

  • The Mauryan period (4th–2nd century BCE) is best known for its highly polished stone sculptures, especially the Ashokan pillars and animal capitals (like the famous Lion Capital of Sarnath). The Mauryan artists worked primarily with Chunar sandstone, a fine-grained, buff-colored stone from near Varanasi.
  • Mauryan art is noted for its mirror-like polish and symbolic simplicity rather than for naturalism. While Mauryan sculptors achieved great mastery in stone finishing, they did not use green schist. Their medium was sandstone, not schist or granite.

Mathura Sculptures :

  • The Mathura School of Art developed around the 2nd century BCE to 3rd century CE in the region of Uttar Pradesh, particularly Mathura. It represented the indigenous Indian style of sculpture — robust, sensuous, and spiritually expressive.
  • The Mathura artists used reddish or pinkish spotted sandstone, obtained locally from the Sikri quarries near Mathura. The sculptures from this school often depict Buddha, Jain Tirthankaras, and Hindu deities with round faces, thick bodies, and smooth modeling, reflecting vitality and earthly warmth.

Bharhut Sculptures :

  • The Bharhut Stupa in Madhya Pradesh represents early Sunga period (2nd century BCE) Buddhist art. It is famous for its railing and gateway sculptures, which depict scenes from the Jataka tales, symbolic motifs, and narrative reliefs.
  • The stone used in Bharhut sculptures was reddish-brown sandstone, a local material. The carvings are more linear and detailed, with figures that are symbolic rather than naturalistic. The use of schist was unknown in this school — the artists relied on sandstone typical of Central India.

Gandhara Sculptures :

  • The Gandhara School of Art (1st century BCE – 5th century CE) flourished under the Kushanas, particularly Kanishka, in regions covering northwestern India and Afghanistan — mainly Taxila, Peshawar, and Bamiyan.
  • The Gandhara artists combined Indian religious themes (especially Buddhism) with Greek and Roman artistic techniques — a style known as Greco-Buddhist art. The figures display naturalistic human anatomy, wavy hair, calm expressions, and flowing drapery reminiscent of Greek sculptures.
  • Importantly, Gandhara sculptures were carved predominantly out of green schist (chlorite schist) and sometimes gray-blue mica schist, available in the Swat and Peshawar regions. This stone gave the sculptures a distinctive smoothness and durability, ideal for fine detailing.
  • Common themes included the life of the Buddha, Bodhisattvas, and other Buddhist motifs, depicted with exceptional realism.

Thus, the correct answer is D.

question: Which one of the following pairs is correctly matched? (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Naqqual : Bihar
option_2: Tamasha : Orissa
option_3: Ankia Nat : Assam
option_4: Baha : Punjab
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

The Naqqal :

  • They are a Muslim community in India, found in the states of Delhi and Uttar Pradesh. They are also known as the Kashmiri Bhand and Kashmiri Shaikh. The Naqqal are a sub-group of the larger Bhand community.

Tamasha :

  • It is a traditional form of Marathi theater that involves singing, dancing, skits, mimicry, poetry, and farce. It is a popular folk performance from Maharashtra, India that combines erotic themes and double entendres. Tamasha often expresses social and political themes.
  • Tamasha is an erotic form of Indian folk drama that began in the early 18th century in Maharashtra. In other forms of Indian folk theater, men are cast in the major roles, but in tamasha, the leading female role is played by a woman.

Ankia Nat :

  • It is a one-act play that originated in Assam, India. It is performed in the style of an opera and depicts events from the life of Krishna. The plays are written in a language called Brajavali, which is an artificial Assamese Maithili mixed language.
  • Ankia Nat is said to have been invented by the medieval saint and social reformer Srimanta Sankardeva and his disciple Mahadeva in the 16th century.
  • The plays are a combination of pre-modern Assamese puppet dances, Ozha-Pali, and other Indian theatrical institutions. They also incorporate Sanskrit play techniques and practices.
  • Ankia Nat plays typically combine live instruments, singers, dance, and elaborate costumes. In Assam, Ankia Nat is also known as Bhaona.

Baha :

  • The Bahá’í teachings offer a perspective on art and its practice. The teachings say that art is a reflection of God’s names, the Fashioner, and the creative process. Bahá’ís believe that creating art is an act of worship. T hey also say that true art is a reflection of divine inspiration.
  • The Bahá’í community values artistic expression. They encourage artists to help ennoble the human soul and to give creative expression to their beliefs. Bahá’ís also believe that gifted people in music, drama, and visual arts can exercise their talents in ways that serve the community.

question: Which one of the following pairs is correctly matched? (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Guru Amar Das : Miri and Piri
option_2: Guru Arjan Dev : Adi Granth
option_3: Guru Ram Das : Dal Khalsa
option_4: Guru Gobind Singh : Manji
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The correct pair is Guru Arjan Dev and Adi Granth.
  • Guru Arjan Dev, the fifth Guru of Sikhism, compiled the Adi Granth, also known as the Guru Granth Sahib. Guru Arjan Devlived from 1563–1606 and was executed by the Emperor.

Miri and Piri :

  • They are two swords worn by the sixth Sikh Guru, Hargobind Singh Ji.

The swords represent temporal and spiritual power, respectively:

  •  Miri: Comes from the Perso-Arabic word “Amir” or “Emir” and means po litical power.
  •  Piri: Comes from the Perso-Arabic word “Pir” and means spiritual power.
  • The concept of “The Mir and the Pir” (temporal power and spiritual authority) was introduced by Hargobind, who is also known as Miri Piri de Malik, meaning “the King of the temporal and spiritual powers”.
  • The concept of Miri-Piri is a political spiritual doctrine in Sikhism that is rooted in both the worldly and the timeless, and in sovereignty beyond the nation-states.

Dal Khalsa :

  • It was the name of the combined military forces of 11 Sikh misls that operated in the 18th century (1735–1799) in the Punjab region. The military force was established by Nawab Kapur Singh in the mid 1730s.

Manji :

  • A Sikh religious administrative unit, similar to a parish or diocese, founded by the third Guru of Sikhism, Guru Amar Das. The Manji system is a system where Guru Amar Das divided the spiritual realm into 22 parts, called Manji, and appoints a Sikh to preach sermons in each part.

question: The Nagara, the Dravida and the Vesara are.............. (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: The three main racial groups of the Indian subcontinent
option_2: The three main linguistic divisions into which the languages of India can be classified
option_3: The three main styles of Indian temple architecture
option_4: The three main musical gharanas prevalent in India
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Nagara, Dravida, and Vesara are three main styles of Hindu temple architecture in India.

The Nagara style :

  • It is a Hindu style of temple architecture that originated in northern India. It’s popular in Northern, Western, and Eastern India, especially in the regions around Malwa, Rajputana, and Kalinga.

Here are some features of the Nagara style:

1. Raised plinth :

  • The temple is built on a raised stone platform with steps leading up to it.

2. Garbha griha

  • The garbha griha, or sanctum sanctorum, is the most sacred part of the temple. It’s a perfect square and houses the image or idol of the main deity.

3. Shikhara :

  • The most distinguishable aspect of Nagara style temples is the towering shikhara, or “mountain peak”, that towers over the garbha griha. The earliest temples have just one shikhara, but later temples have several. The garbhagriha is always located directly under the tallest tower.

4. Kalasha :

  • The shikhara is topped by a bulbous finial called a kalasha.

5. Plan :

  • The plan of a Nagara style of temple is four sided, but the whole temple plan could be oblong.

6. Subdivisions :

  • There are many subdivisions of nagara temples depending on the shape of the shikhara.

Here are some examples of Nagara style temples:

1. Kandariya Mahadeva Temple: Located in Khajuraho, Madhya Pradesh

2. Jagdish Temple: Located in Udaipur, Rajasthan

3. Khajuraho temple complex: A UNESCO World Heritage Site

4. Konark Sun Temple: A UNESCO World Heritage Site

5. Mahabodhi Temple: A UNESCO World Heritage Site

6. Sun Temple in Modhera, Gujarat: An example of Nagara-style temple architecture

7. Ossian Temple in Gujarat: An exam ple of Nagara-style temple architecture

Dravidian architecture :

  • It is a style of temple architecture that originated in South India during the Gupta period. It’s one of the three main styles of Indian temple architecture, along with the Nagara and Vesara styles.

Here are some features of Dravidian architecture:

1. Compound wall: The temple is sur rounded by a compound wall.

2. Gopuram: The entrance gateway is located in the center of the front wall.

3. Vimana: The main temple tower is a stepped pyramid that rises geomet rically. The vimana is also known as the gopuram or shikhara. It’s typically adorned with intricate carvings and sculptures, and often has multiple tiers.

4. Pillars and pilasters: The temple has pillars and pilasters.

5. Curved top: The temple has a curved top.

6. Boundary walls: The temple has boundary walls.

7. Water tank: The temple enclosure has a water tank.

8. Panchayatan style: The temple grounds are designed in the pancha yatan style, with a main temple and four smaller shrines.

  • Some examples of Dravidian temples include the Brihadeeshwara temple at Thanjavur and the Gangaikondacholapuram temple.

The Vesara style of temple architecture :

  • It is a combination of the Dravidian and Nagara styles of temple architecture. It’s a hybrid form of Indian temple architecture, with the plan and shape of South Indian temples and the details of North Indian temples.
  • The Vesara style is said to have originated in the mid-seventh century A.D. with the later Chalukya rulers. The word “Vesara” is derived from the Sanskrit word vishra, which means “an area to walk for a long time”.

Some features of the Vesara style include:

1. Dravidian vimana

2. Nagara-type faceted walls

3. Selective merging of the

4. Nagara and Dravida orders

5. A star-shaped plan

6. Projecting angles in circles with centers in the middle of the shrine and mandap

7. Pillars, doorways, ceilings are decorated with intricate carvings

8. A usually pyramidal tower, similar to the Dravida style, but may lack the stepped appearance seen in pure Dravida architecture

  • Some famous temples of Vesara style are the Kailashnath temple, Ellora, Chennakesava temple, Virupaksha temple, and Ladkhan temple.

question: According to the Mimamsa system of philosophy, liberation is possible by means of.............. (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Jnana
option_2: Bhakti
option_3: Yoga
option_4: Karma
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • According to the Mimamsa system of philosophy, liberation is possible through karma.
  • The Mimamsa system of philosophy is based on the Mimamsa Sutras of sage Jaimini. It believes that the Vedas are eternal and authoritative, and that performing Vedic rituals is the way to achieve liberation.
  • The Mimamsa system holds that every action has a consequence, and that one’s present life is a result of one’s past actions. Therefore, one must perform actions in accordance with dharma to ensure a good future and ultimately attain liberation.
  • The goal of Mimamsa is to provide enlightenment on dharma. Dharma is understood as the set of ritual obligations and prerogatives that, if properly performed, maintains the harmony of the world and furthers the personal goals of the performer.

question: The Mughal School of Painting formed the spinal column of the various schools of Indian miniature art. Which one of the following painting styles was not affected by Mughal painting? (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Pahari
option_2: Rajasthani
option_3: Kangra
option_4: Kalighata
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Kalighata painting style was not affected by Mughal painting.
  • The Mughal School of Miniature Painting was a significant development in the 16th century in the northern Indian subcontinent. The school was a combination of Persian and Indian miniature painting styles. It is considered a landmark in the history of Indian painting.

The Mughal School of Painting influenced many Indian miniature art schools, including:

1. Pala School of Miniature Painting

2. Jaina School of Miniature Painting

3. Rajasthan School of Miniature Painting

4. Orissa School of Miniature Painting

5. Pahari School of Miniature Painting

6. Deccan School of Miniature Painting

Kalighat paintings :

  • They are a traditional Indian art form that originated in Kolkata in the 19th century. They are known for their bright colors, bold outlines, and stylized elongated eyes.
  • Kalighat paintings are a religious art form that depicts mythological stories, figures of Hindu gods and goddesses, and scenes from everyday life and society. The paintings are made on mill-paper treated with a paste of lime, on which watercolors are dabbed using a large brush or a rag.
  • Kalighat paintings are also known as Kalighat Patachitra and are developed by Jamini Roy. The paintings were originally made to be sold as souvenirs to people who came to the Kalighat Temple.

Some famous Kalighat painting artists include:

  1. Bhawanipur Sitaldas: Known for his portraits of women and scenes from everyday life
  2. Gobardhan Bhattacharya: Known for his depictions of the gods and goddesses of the Hindu pantheon, as well as scenes from mythology

Pahari painting :

  • It is a style of miniature painting and book illustration that originated in the Himalayan foothills of India between the 17th and 19th centuries. The word “pahari” comes from the Hindi word pahar, which means “mountain”. Pahari paintings are similar in style and color to Rajput paintings.

Pahari paintings are characterized by:

1. Delicate lines

2. Vibrant colors like yellow, red, and blue

3. Individualistic characteristics in painting

4. Depiction of nature, architecture, figural types, facial features, and costumes

5. Preference for particular colors

  • Pahari paintings are closely related to Rājasthanī painting and share with the Rājput art of the North Indian plains a preference for depicting legends.

Pahari paintings include:

1. Radha and Lord Krishna

2. Jayadev’s ‘Gita-Govinda’

3. Bhagavata Purana

4. Two Madhumalati manuscripts

5. Shangri Ramayana

6. Jasrota paintings

Rajasthani painting :

  • It is a style of miniature painting that developed in the 16th–19th century in the independent Hindu states of Rajasthan in western India. It evolved from Western Indian manuscript illustrations, but Mughal influence became evident in the later years of its development.
  • Rajasthani paintings are most representative of the Hindu Rajputana and are also called Hindu style. They successfully depict the religious beliefs of the people of that time as well as it portrays the insights of the social life of the people at the time of medieval period.

There are three kinds of Rajasthani paintings:

1. Phad: A scroll painting done on long cloth panels depicting folk and his toric stories in a narrative style. Phad paintings are created on hand-woven coarse cotton cloth, which is soaked overnight to thicken the threads.

2. Pichwai: Done on cloth.

3. Miniature: Done on specially treated paper in vegetable dyes.

  • Rajasthani paintings are usually made in tempera medium, or water color. The painting style uses semi precious stones like agate, blood stone, amethyst, citrine, carnelian, jade, tiger, malachite, and others.These semi precious stones are carefully selected, and grounded to a paste to act as paint.

Kangra paintings :

  • They are a type of Pahari painting that are named after the former princely state of Kangra in Himachal Pradesh.

Kangra paintings are characterized by:

1. Soft colors, including cool blues and greens

2. Lyrical treatment of themes

3. Identical style to the portraits of Raja Sansar Chand of Kangra

question: Zero was invented by............. (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Aryabhata
option_2: Varahmira
option_3: Bhaskara 1
option_4: An unknown Indian
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The concept of zero as a digit is believed to have originated in India in the 5th century. The mathematician and astronomer Aryabhata is often associated with inventing the number zero.
  • In the 7th century, Brahmagupta developed the earliest known methods for using zero in calculations. He named zero “shunya” or “sunya”, which is the Sanskrit word for emptiness or nothingness.
  • Brahmagupta also demonstrated that zero can be reached through calculation. He wrote these rules in his book Brahmasphutasiddhanta. Brahmagupta and others used small dots under numbers to show a zero placeholder. They also viewed the zero as having a null value, called “sunya”. Brahmagupta was also the first to show that subtracting a number from itself results in zero.

Varahamihira (505–587) :

  • He was an Indian astronomer, mathematician, and astrologer. He was also known as Varaha or Mihira.
  • Varahamihira is known for writing the Pancha-Siddhantikaa (“Five Treatises”), a compilation of Greek, Egyptian, Roman, and Indian astronomy. He also discovered a version of Pascal’s triangle and worked on magic squares.

Varahamihira’s mathematical accomplishments include:

1. Discovering trigonometric formulas

2. Improving the precision of Aryabhata’s sine tables

3. Defining the algebraic properties of zero and negative numbers

  • Varahamihira was also a physician. Some mathematicians believed he had a strong knowledge of predictive calculations and zodiac signs. He is also known for predicting that there was water on Mars.

Bhaskara I (600 - 680) :

  • He was an Indian astronomer and mathematician who lived around 629. He is considered the most important scholar of Aryabhata’s astronomical school.

Bhaskara I is known for:

1. Helping to spread the mathematical work of Aryabhata

2. Being the first mathematician to use the Hindu decimal method to write numbers

3. Working on the Sine Function, giving a more accurate value for Sine

4. Authoring two treatises and commentaries to the work of Aryabhata I

5. His works include the Mahabhaskariya, the Laghubhaskariya, and the Aryabhatiyabhasya

  • Bhaskara I is considered one of the most renowned Indian mathematicians, along with Brahmagupta. Both made significant contributions to the study of fractions.
  • Bhaskara I is also the name of India’s first experimental remote sensing satellite, built by ISRO. It was named after the 7th century Indian mathematician Bhaskara-I, not to be confused with the 12th-century mathematician Bhaskara-II.

question: Bronze icons of Nataraja cast during the Chola period invariably show the deity with............ (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: eight hands
option_2: six hands
option_3: four hands
option_4: two hands
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Bronze icons of Nataraja cast during the Chola period invariably show the deity with four hands. The four hands of Nataraja hold significant symbols that represent various aspects of Lord Shiva’s power and cosmic dance.
  • Nataraja is a depiction of the Hindu god Shiva as the cosmic ecstatic dancer. The figure appears to be performing a cosmic dance, with his arms and legs in different poses and his hair and clothing flowing behind him.
  • The Nataraja is widely regarded as the pinnacle of Chola bronze art, revered for its dynamic movement and intricate details.

question: Which one of the following monuments has a dome which is said to be one of the largest in the world? (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Tomb of Sher Shah, Sasaram
option_2: Jama Masjid, Delhi
option_3: Tomb of Ghiyas-ud-din Tughlaq, Delhi
option_4: Gol Gumbaz, Bijapur
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Gol Gumbaz in Bijapur, India is said to have one of the largest domes in the world. It is the largest dome in India and the fourth largest in the world. The dome is so large that it doesn’t need pillars for support.
  • The Gol Gumbaz is a 17th-century mausoleum that houses the remains of Mohammad Adil Shah, the seventh sultan of the Adil Shahi dynasty. The architect Yaqut of Dabul completed the tomb in 1656.

The Gol Gumbaz has several notable features, including:

Echoes :

  • The dome’s acoustics are such that every sound echoes seven times in the central chamber.

Whispering Gallery :

  • The Whispering Gallery is a space where even the faintest sounds can be heard clearly from 37 meters away.

Shape :

  • The dome is perfectly hemispherical, which allows sound waves to pass through it unhindered.

The tomb of Sher Shah Suri :

  • It is located in Sasaram, Bihar, India. It was commissioned by the emperor himself and completed in 1545, about three months after his death. The tomb is a mausoleum that is considered a majestic example of ancient architecture. It features elements of Indo-Islamic architecture, including high domes, pillars, and large open courtyards, as well as hints of Afghan architecture.

Jama Masjid :

  • It is also known as Masjid-i-Jahanuma, is a mosque in Old Delhi, India. It was built between 1644 and 1656 by Mughal emperor Shah Jahan, who also built the Taj Mahal and the Red Fort.
  • Jama Masjid is the largest mosque in India and can accommodate 25,000 people in its courtyard. It is an example of Mughal architecture and is made of red sandstone and marble. The mosque was the imperial mosque of the Mughal emperors until the end of the empire in 1857.

The Tomb of Ghiyas-ud-din Tughlaq :

  • It is a fortress-like complex that dates back to the 1300s. It’s located in southern Delhi, near Tughlaqabad Fort. The tomb is set in a pentagonal enclosure surrounded by high walls.
  • The tomb was originally surrounded by a pond and connected to the fort by a causeway. The causeway is 229 meters long and is supported by 26 arched piers. The tomb is now separated from the fort by a dry artificial lake.
  • The tomb is entered through a high red sandstone gateway with steps. The mausoleum is a single-domed square tomb that’s about 8 meters by 8 meters. The walls are sloping and have parapets. The western side has three elaborately carved minarets. The 30-foot square hall’s walls are decorated with white marble and sandstone.
  • The tomb has underground cells that are accessed from a peripheral corridor. The cells are now guarded by wire mesh doors for security.

question: Which one of the following dances involves solo performance? (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: Bharatanatyam
option_2: Kuchipudi
option_3: Mohiniattam
option_4: Odissi
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Mohiniattam, the classical dance of Kerala, is known for its solo female performances marked by graceful movements and soft expressions. The term means “dance of the enchantress,” and it depicts themes of love and devotion, especially to Lord Vishnu in his Mohini form.
  • Bharatanatyam (Tamil Nadu) and Odissi (Odisha) can also be performed solo but often include group recitals. Kuchipudi (Andhra Pradesh) originated as a dance-drama performed by groups. 
  • Hence, the dance form traditionally involving solo performance is Mohiniattam.

question: Which one of the following pairs of composers in different languages and their works on the Mahabharata theme is correctly matched? (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: Sarladasa - Bengali
option_2: Kasirama - Oriya
option_3: Tikkana - Marathi
option_4: Pampa - Kannada
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The correct answer is Pampa and Kannada.
  • Pampa was a well-known Kannada poet who worked on the Mahabharata theme. His works reflected his philosophical beliefs, and his Vikramarjuna Vijaya, also known as Pampa Bharata, is a Kannada version of the Mahabharata of Vyasa.

Here are some other composers and their works on the Mahabharata theme:

Saraladasa :

  •  A 15th-century poet and scholar of Odia literature, best known for three Odia books: Sarala Mahabharata, Vilanka Ramayana, and Chandi Purana.

Tikkana :

  • A Telugu poet, part of the Kavitrayam, a group of poets who translated the Mahabharata into Telugu.

Kavitrayam :

  • A group of poets who translated the Mahabharata into Telugu, including Nannayya, Tikkana, and Yerrapragada.

question: The famous dialogue between, Nachiketa and Yama is mentioned in the............. (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: Channdogyapanishad
option_2: Mundkapanishad
option_3: Kathopanishad
option_4: Kenopanishad
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The famous dialogue between Nachiketa and Yama is mentioned in the Kathopanishad. The Kathopanishad is one of the 108 Upanishads and consists of two chapters, each divided into three sections. The first chapter is considered to be older than the second.
  • The story of Nachiketa and Yama is a legendary one. Nachiketa is the son of Sage Vajasravasa, who meets Yama, the deity of death. The dialogue between Nachiketa and Yama is about the transmigration of the soul.
  • The story of Nachiketa and Yama is a powerful one that has been told and retoldfor centuries. It is a story that teaches us about the importance of life and death, and the journey of the soul.

The Chandogya Upanishad :

  • It is a Sanskrit religious text that is part of the Chandogya Brahmana of the Sama Veda of Hinduism. It is one of the oldest Upanishads, and is the second largest in size, after the Brhadaranyaka Upanishad.
  • The Chandogya Upanishad is a philosophical exposition on the relationship between the self (atman) and the universe (brahman). It is a representation of the ancient Indian school of philosophy, spirituality, and knowledge. The story of the Chandogya Upanishad tells of a father teaching his son about the ultimate truth of Hinduism.

The Mundaka Upanishad :

  • It is an ancient philosophical text written in Sanskrit and Vedic Sanskrit. It is part of the Atharva Veda and is one of the most widely translated Upanishads.
  • The Mundaka Upanishad is a collection of philosophical poems that teach meditation and spiritual knowledge. It is presented as a dialogue between sage Saunaka and sage Angiras.

The Upanishad is divided into three sections:

1. First part: Discusses higher and low er knowledge

2. Second part: Describes the true nature of the Self and Brahman, and the path to knowing Brahman

3. Third part: Expands on the first two sections

  • The word Mundaka is Sanskrit and means “shaved” or “shorn”. It may refer to knowledge that shaves away ignorance.
