  • The Mundaka Upanishad describes Brahman as the source of all creation, eternal, and beyond the grasp of the limited human intellect. Understanding and realizing the nature of Brahman is central to spiritual growth and self-realization.

The Kathopanishad :

  • It is an ancient Hindu text written in Sanskrit. It is one of the mukhya Upanishads, which are part of the Vedas. The Kathopanishad is part of the last eight short sections of the Katha school of the Krishna Yajurveda. It is also known as Kāthaka Upanishad and is listed as number 3 in the Muktika canon of 108 Upanishads.
  • The Kathopanishad is a mystical text that offers insights into the nature of life, death, and the eternal quest for knowledge. It is a narrative-style text that captivates readers with its timeless wisdom. The Kathopanishad begins with a katha, or story, of a young boy who is ready to face the Lord of Death in his quest for Truth. The main theme of one of the songs of Sri Swami Sivanandaji Maharaj is “Die to live”.

The Kenopanishad :

  • It is a Vedic Sanskrit text that is part of the Sama Veda. It is listed as the second Muktikā, the canon of the 108 Upanishads of Hinduism.
  • The Kenopanishad is a philosophical dialogue between a student and a spiritual teacher. It explores the nature of Brahman, the ultimate reality, and the means to attain self-realization and transcendence. The Kenopanishad also describes an incident in the lives of celestial beings that teaches a significant lesson.
  • The Kenopanishad deals with the nature of the highest reality, Brahman, and establishes that this highest reality is not what can come within the scope of our ordinary knowledge.

question: Which one of the following scripts of ancient India was written from right to left? (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: Brahmi
option_2: Nandnagari
option_3: Sharada
option_4: Kharoshti
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

The Kharosthi script :

  • It was written from right to left and is one of the ancient scripts of India. It was developed between the 4th and 3rd century BCE in present-day northern Pakistan. The Kharosthi script was used to represent a form of Prakrit, an Indo-Aryan language. It is also known as the “Indo-Bactrian” script.
  • The Kharosthi script is associated with Aramaic and is a sister script of Brahmi. Some inscriptions show the Kharosthi script written from left to right.

The Brahmi script :

  • It is a writing system that originated in ancient India and is still used today in parts of Asia. It is an abugida, which uses diacritical marks to connect vowels to consonant symbols.
  • The Brahmi script is the ancestor of many modern Indian scripts, including Devanagari. It is also the ancestor of hundreds of scripts in Southeast and East Asia.
  • The Brahmi script is believed to have originated in the 8th or 7th century BC and may have been introduced to India by Semitic people. One theory is that Brahman scholars adapted the Semitic script to fit the phonetics of Sanskrit and Prakrit.
  • The Brahmi script is semialphabetic, with each consonant having either an inherent “a” sound or a diacritic mark to indicate another vowel. Initial vowels have separate characters.
  • The first phase of the Brahmi script is known as Aśokan Brāhmī and was used from about the 3rd century BCE to the end of the first century BCE.

Nandinagari :

  • It is a Brahmic script that is a derivative of the Nāgarī script, which appeared in the 7th century AD. The script was used in South India from the 8th to 19th centuries to write Sanskrit manuscripts and inscriptions about philosophy, science, and the arts.
  • Nandinagari is closely related to the Devanagari script. The Nāgarī scriptevolved into Devanagari and Nandinagari scripts by the end of the first millennium CE.
  • The Nāgarī script has roots in the ancient Brahmi script family.
  • The earliest evidence of using modern standardized Devanagari is presumed to exist by around 1000 CE.

The Sharada script :

  • It is an ancient writing system that was used to write Sanskrit and Kashmiri languages between the 8th and 12th centuries. It is a descendant of the Brahmi script and is slightly similar to the Devanagari script.
  • The Sharada script originated in the 8th century AD and evolved from the Gupta script of North India. The earliest inscriptions in Sharada script are dated to 804 AD and were found in Kashmir and northeastern Punjab.
  • The Sharada script is taught in Hindu schools in Kashmir but is not used in printing books. Today, very few people of the Kashmiri Pandit community use Sharada for religious texts. However, pundits in Kashmir still use the script to make birth horoscopes.

question: Milindapanho is in the form of a dialogue between the King Menander and the Buddhist monk............ (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: Nagasena
option_2: Nagarjuna
option_3: Nagabhatta
option_4: Kumarilabhatta
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Milindapanho is a Buddhist text that is a dialogue between King Menander and the Buddhist monk Nagasena. In the text, King Menander asks questions about Buddhist doctrine and dilemmas, and Nagasena answers them. The Milindapanho is regarded as canonical in Burmese Buddhism, included as part of the book of Khuddaka Nikāya.
  • An abridged version is included as part of Chinese Mahāyāna translations of thecanon. The Milindapanho is not regarded as canonical by Thai or Sri Lankan Buddhism, however, despite the surviving Theravāda text being in Sinhalese script.
  • The Chinese text titled the Monk Nāgasena Sutra corresponds to the first three chapters of the Milindapanha. It was translated sometime during the Eastern Jin dynasty.

question:
Which of the following pairs are correctly matched? (UPSC Prelims 1998)

1.  Mrichchakatikam

Shudraka

2.  Buddhacharita

Vasuvandhu

3. Mudrarakshasha

Vishakhadatta

4. Harshacharita

 Banahhatta

 

 

 

 

 

Select the correct answer using the codes given below.

Codes:
option_1: 1, 2, 3 and 4
option_2: 1, 3 and 4
option_3: 1 and 4
option_4: 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Śūdraka, an Indian king and playwright, wrote Mrichchhakatika (The Little Clay Cart) in the 2nd century BCE. It is considered one of the earliest Indian plays written in Sanskrit.
  • Mrichchhakatika is about a young man named Charudatta and his love for Vasantasena, a rich courtesan. The play explores universal aspects of human nature, such as love, desire, morality, and the complexities of relationships. It also offers a window into the cultural and societal norms of ancient India.
  • The play is filled with characters like gamblers, thieves, and courtesans. According to the play, it is not the social status of the person that makes them noble, rather it is their virtue and behavior.
  • Horace Hayman Wilson, a surgeon general in the East India Company, translated Mrichchhakatika into English in 1826. It was later translated into French, German, and other Indian languages.
  • Aśvaghosa wrote Buddhacharita in the early second century CE. The poem is a Sanskrit mahakavya style epic about the life of Gautama Buddha. The title translates to "Acts of the Buddha".

Asvaghosa : 

  • Asvaghosa was a Buddhist philosopher, dramatist, poet, musician, and orator from India. He is believed to have been the first Sanskrit dramatist and is considered the greatest Indian poet before Kālidāsa.
  • Buddhacharita is a poetic narrative of the Buddha's life and is considered one of the finest examples of Buddhist literature. The poem is made up of 28 cantons and describes the Buddha's life from birth to death. The poem begins with a description of the Buddha's birth.

Vishakhadatta : 

  • Vishakhadatta wrote the Sanskrit play Satta Mudrarakshasa. The title translates to "The Signet of the Minister". The play is about the rise to power of King Chandragupta Maurya in India. It is a historical play that provides insights into the political situation in ancient India during the reign of Chandragupta Maurya.
  • The play is set between the 4th and 8th centuries BC. It tells the story of how Chandragupta Maurya ascended the throne of Magadha after defeating the Nandas. The title refers to the signet ring of Rakshasa, which is stolen by Chanakya's spy. Chanakya uses the ring to forge a letter that sows suspicion between Rakshasa and Malayaketu.

Banabhatta :

  • He is also known as Bana, wrote Harshacharita in the 7th century CE. Banabhatta was a Sanskrit writer and the court poet of the Indian emperor Harsha. Harshacharita is a biography of Harsha, and its title translates to "The deeds of Harsha".
  • Harshacharita is written in an elaborate style of poetic prose, with eight chapters that describe the emperor's life. The biography provides valuable information about the period, though some say it exaggerates in the emperor's favor.
  • Banabhatta also wrote Kadambari and Parvathy Parinayam.

question: he first writer to use Urdu as the medium of poetic expression was............. (UPSC Prelims 1999)
option_1: Amir Khusrau
option_2: Mirza Ghalib
option_3: Bahadur Shah Zafar
option_4: Faiz
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Amir Khusrau (1253-1325 CE) is considered the first writer to use Urdu as a medium of poetic expression. He was a Persian poet, musician, and scholar associated with the rulers of the Delhi Sultanate.
  • Khusrau is also known as the “Father of Urdu Literature”. He played a significant role in the development and popularization of Urdu language and literature. Khusrau’s poetry is still sung today at Sufi shrines throughout Pakistan.
  • Khusrau also wrote poetry in Persian and Hindi. He contributed immensely to the development of Hindavi, one of the predecessor dialects of modern Urdu.

question:
Consider the following statements: (UPSC Prelims 1999)

The striking features of the Jama Masjid in Kashmir, completed by Zain-ul-Abidin, include (s)

(a) turret

(b) similarity with Buddhist pagodas

(c) Persian style

Which of the above statements is/are correct?
option_1: 1 alone
option_2: 1, 2 and 3
option_3: 2 and 3
option_4: 1 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

The Correct Answer is (B), 1 2 3.

  • Jama Masjid is a mosque in Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir, India.
  • The Jama Masjid of Srinagar is situated at Nowhatta, in the middle of the old city.
  • It was built by Sultan Sikandar in 1400 AD. Later, the son of Sultan Sikandar, Zain-ul-Abidin had the mosque extended.

question:
Which one of the following pairs of folk dance forms and states is not correctly matched? (UPSC Prelims 1999)

(a) Korku

 Maharashtra

(b) Jhummar

 Haryana

(c) Thali

 Himachal Pradesh

(d) Mukna

 Manipur
option_1:
Korku

 Maharashtra
option_2:
Jhummar

 Haryana
option_3:
Thali

 Himachal Pradesh
option_4:
Mukna

 Manipur
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

Folk dance:

  • It is a type of dance that expresses a expression. It is a way to connect with and culture's past or present through vernacular express a group's cultural roots.
  • Folk dances are often performed at festivals, cultural gatherings, or agricultural events, They are usually performed by people without professional dance training and to traditional folk music.
  • Folk dances are not a universal genre of dance. They have no universal movement, figure, form, style, or function when compared from one culture to another.

The Korku tribe :

  • They performs the Chatkora and Gadli dances in Madhya Pradesh. The Korku are a Munda ethnic group that live in the Khandwa, Burhanpur, Betul, and Chhindwara districts of Madhya Pradesh. They also live in the adjoining areas near the Melghat Tiger Reserve of Maharashtra.

The Jhumar dance :

  • It is a popular folk dance from the state of Haryana, India. The dance is also known as the "Hariyanvi Gidda" and is mainly performed by young married women. The dance is named after the Jhumar ornament and is performed to the beats of instruments like the dholak and thali.
  • The Jhumar dance often depicts romantic themes, with performers expressing and delicate gestures. This style of Jhumar dance is particularly popular during weddings and other joyous occasions.
  • The Jhumar dance originated from Balochistan and Multan. 
  • Other folk dances from Haryana include: Ras Leela, Phalgun Dance, Ghumar Dance.

The Thali dance :

  • It is a folk dance from Jammu and Kashmir. Artists perform the traditional Thali dance in the Ramban district of Jammu and Kashmir.

Terah Taali dance :

  • The Terah Taali dance is a folk dance from Rajasthan. The name Terah Taali means "13 claps". The Kamad community performs this dance, mainly women who sit on the ground while performing.

Mukna :

  • It is a folk wrestling dance from the state of Manipur in India. It's a combination of wrestling and judo, and is popular in Imphal, Thoubal, and Bishnupur. Mukna is often played on the final day of the Lai Haraoba festival, which is part of its ceremonial functions.

question: The paintings of Abanindranath Tagore are classified as............ (UPSC Prelims 1999)
option_1: realistic
option_2: socialistic
option_3: revivalistic
option_4: impressionistic
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Abanindranath Tagore’s paintings are classified as revivalistic. Tagore is considered the leader of the revivalist movement in modern Indian painting in Bengal. His work is considered the foundation of realism.
  • Tagore’s paintings are considered revivalist art, which led to the creation of the Bengal School of Art. The Bengal School was a movement that sought to revive traditional Indian artistic practices and create a unique national identity.
  • Tagore is considered a father figure of nationalist and modernist art in India. He revived aspects of Indian and oriental painting traditions, and invented the wash painting technique.

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists:  (UPSC Prelims 1999)

List-1

 List-II

A. Pandit Vishnu Digambar Paluskar

 1. Introduced the scheme of Raga classification in Indian Music

B. Venkatamahi

 2. Proponent of the Carnatic music

C. Shyama Shastri

 3. Proponent of the Khayal form of Hindustani music

D. Amir Khusrau

 4. Wrote the music for the song “Vande Mataram

Codes :

A B C D
option_1: 4 1 3 2
option_2: 4 1 2 3
option_3: 1 4 3 2
option_4: 1 4 2 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Pandit Vishnu Digambar Paluskar popularized Hindustani music and composed the tune for “Vande Mataram.”
  • Venkatamahi (Venkatamakhin) introduced the Melakarta raga classification system in Indian music.
  • Shyama Shastri, one of the Trinity of Carnatic music, enriched South Indian classical tradition with devotional compositions.
  • Amir Khusrau, the Sufi poet-musician, pioneered the Khayal form of Hindustani music and blended Persian and Indian elements.

Hence, the correct matching is A–4, B–1, C–2, D–3.

question: Which one of the following statements is not correct? (UPSC Prelims 1999)
option_1: Neel Darpan was a play based on the exploitation of the indigo farmers
option_2: The author of the play ‘Ghashiram Kotwal’ is Vijay Tendulkar
option_3: The play Navanna’ by Nabin Chandra Das was based on the famine of Bengal
option_4: Urdu theatre used to depend heavily on Parsi theatre
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Neel Darpan is a Bengali play written by Dinabandhu Mitra between 1858 and 1859. The play is also known as The Indigo Planting Mirror and is considered a significant text of 19th century sociopolitical and literary concerns.
  • The play is set during the Indigo Revolt of February–March 1859, when farmers in Bengal refused to sow indigo in their fields to protest exploitative farming practices under the British Raj. The play was published in Dhaka in 1860 under a pseudonym.
  • The play is said to have moved Reverend James Long, a clergyman from the Church Missionary Society, who translated it into English and published it. Long was then tried by the English government and sentenced to one month in jail for his protest against the exploitation of indigo farmers.
  • Vijay Tendulkar wrote the 1972 Marathi play Ghashiram Kotwal. The play is a political satire and historical drama that explores the theme of how powerful men create ideologies to serve their own purposes, and then destroy them when they are no longer useful.
  • Ghashiram Kotwal is set in Maharashtra in the late 18th century and tells the story of a power struggle based on caste ascendancy in politics. The play is based on the lives of Nana Phadnavis, a prominent minister in the court of the Peshwa of Pune, and Ghashiram Kotwal, the city’s police chief.
  • Tendulkar (1928–2008) was a pioneer of modern Marathi plays. His works are known for their strong socio-political commentary and for depicting the harsh realities of life.

Some of the themes that appear frequently in his plays include:

1. Gender discrimination

2. Sexual norms

3. Violence

4. Social issues

5. Power and morality

  • Nabanna is a Bengali play written by Bijon Bhattacharya about the 1943 Bengal famine. The play was first staged in 1944 by the Indian People’s Theatre Association (IPTA) and again in 1948 by Bohurupee.
  • The play tells the story of the villagers of Aminpur, who are already struggling with food shortages and land sharks when a tidal wave destroys their crops and homes. The play is considered a watershed moment in Indian theater, marking a departure from commercial theater.
  • In the initial productions, the playwright himself played a character who has gone insane after years of famine and the death of his two sons. Parsi theater influenced the production, organization, and concept of Urdu theater in India.
  • Parsi theater also influenced Gujarati and Hindi theater. Parsi theater absorbed elements of traditional and folk performing arts, such as music, mime, and comic interludes.
  • Parsi theater was also influenced by Indo Persian/Islamicate culture. Until the early 20th century, Urdu was the most commonly used language in Parsi theater.

question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Chakiarkoothu form of dance............ (UPSC Prelims 2000)

1. It is performed by Chakiar caste.

2. It cannot be traditionally witnessed by the higher caste Hindus.

3. Mizhavu is the accompanying instrument.

4. Its theatre form is called koothambalam.

Which of these statements are correct?
option_1: 1, 3 and 4
option_2: 1, 2 and 3
option_3: 2, 3 and 4
option_4: 1, 2 and 4
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Chakiarkoothu is a traditional dance form from Kerala, India. It is a highly refined monologue where the performer narrates episodes from Hindu epics and stories from the Puranas. The dance is accompanied by cymbals and a copper drum called the mizhavu.
  • Chakiarkoothu is believed to have been introduced to Kerala by early Aryan immigrants. It is performed by the members of the Chakiar caste and can only be staged inside temples and witnessed by the Hindus of the higher castes.
  • The costume for Chakiarkoothu includes a special headgear shaped like a snake’s head. The dancer’s body is smeared with a paste of sandalwood and Vibhuthi and his face is made very catchy with vibrant colors.
  • Painkulam Raman Chakyar : (1905-1980) was the first to stage Chakiarkoothu at a function, bringing it to the general public. He was instrumental in getting it included in the curriculum of Kalamandalam.

question: The Raga which is sung early in the morning is............. (UPSC Prelims 2000)
option_1: Todi
option_2: Darbari
option_3: Bhopali
option_4: Bhimpalasi
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The raga that is sung early in the morning is Raga Todi. It is a classical Indian raga that is associated with the early morning hours and is considered to be a serene and meditative raga.
  • Raga Todi is a concert favorite morning raga. It is sung from 8 am to 10 am and has the ability to evoke the same mood of playfulness of a newborn lying in bed content, smiling and playful. In film music, Raga Todi has been largely used in composing songs that depict pathos. Raga Todi is an Aalap oriented Raga. The Swaras express pathos.

question: Who amongst the following Englishmen, first translated Bhagavad-Gita into English? (UPSC Prelims 2001)
option_1: William Jones
option_2: Charles Wilkins
option_3: Alexander Cunningham
option_4: John Marshall
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Charles Wilkins was the first person to translate the Bhagavad Gita into English in 1784. The translation was published in 1785 and is commonly known as Wilkin’s Gita.
  • Wilkins was an English typographer, Orientalist, and founding member of The Asiatic Society. His translation is considered a significant work in the field of Indology and played a key role in introducing Indian philosophy and religion to the West.

The Bhagavad Gita :

  • It is a 700-verse Hindu scripture, written in Sanskrit, that is part of the epic poem Mahabharata. It is also known as the “Song of the Lord”.
  • The Bhagavad Gita is composed of 18 chapters, chapters 23–40 of book 6, called the Bhishma Parva, of the Mahabharata. It is a dialogue between Prince Arjuna and Krishna, an incarnation of the god Vishnu.
  • The Bhagavad Gita is considered one of the most important yogic texts ever written and presents a synthesis of the concept of Dharma, Bhakti, and the Yogic ideals of moksha.
  • The Bhagavad Gita suggests that one should practice austerity in every way, including in body, mind, personal behavior, and dealings with others. It also suggests that the secret to success at work is cultivating a balanced state of mind.
  • The oldest surviving manuscript of the Bhagavad Gita dates back to 1492 and is held at the Bodleian Library of Oxford University, England.

question: In which one of the following cities is the Lingaraja Temple located? (UPSC Prelims 2001)
option_1: Bhubaneswar
option_2: Bijapur
option_3: Kolkata
option_4: Shravanabelagola
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Lingaraja Temple is a Hindu temple in Bhubaneswar, Odisha, India. It’s one of the oldest temples in the city and is a major tourist attraction.
  • The temple is dedicated to Shiva and is built in the Kalinga style of architecture. It’s 55 meters high, with a main tower that’s 180 feet tall. The temple was built in the 11th century by King Jajati Keshari of Soma Vansh.
